description.string: Using GIMP, A Photo Editing Program

item.value: How to increase SEO for your clients

newpage.pagetitle: Using GIMP - Open Files, View Layers

para.b: Here's what you will learn in this tutorial:

list.item: How to use GIMP - a photo editor similar to PhotoShop (without the cost)

newpage.para: This tutorial was created using GIMP Version 2.6.11
Because this is open-source software it is constantly being upgraded and your version may appear slightly different.
You can download the latest version on the SourceForge.net web site: GIMP downloadhttp://www.gimp.org/downloads/. It is available for Windows, Macintosh OS X, and Linux.
You can view the User Manual on the webhttp://docs.gimp.org/en/.
When you first start up GIMP (GNU Image Manipulation Program) the standard tool box will display in the upper left corner of your screen.
pic003.gifYou can change the size and position of this toolbox area any time you want. Just click and drag an edge or click and drag the title bar and move the window where you want it.
If the Toolbox isn't displaying use CTRL B or choose Windows/Toolbox from the menu bar.
There are a lot of options here, but don't let that worry you. Most of them will just sit there and wait for you until you want to use them.
As you click on an item, the options will change in the bottom half of the window.
For example, in this example you can see that the Paintbrush tool is selected. In the lower half you see all the Paintbrush options that are available.

para.b: To open up files to edit select File/Open on the menu bar.

newpage.para: Once an image is open, look along its menu bar and use Windows menu to see the different dialog boxes you can open up. They are like little mini-toolboxes.
You can quickly change the size of an image by using Image/Scale Image from the image menu:

image.alt: Using Image/Scale Image to resize a picture.

newpage.para: When the Scale Image dialog box comes up, make the choices you need. If you want to keep the image in proportion, make sure the lock is on so changing the height or width will automatically adjust the other dimension.

Here I rescaled a photo that was 3216 pixels wide to 200 pixels. Note how the height automatically calculated to 56 pixels. I also changed the resolution from 300 down to 72 to make the image as small as possible for the Web:

image.alt: The Scale Image dialog box showing the locks so the picture proportions stay the same as you change either the length or the width.

newpage.para: Important: You can always shrink/rescale a photo to a smaller size but if you try to make a smaller image larger you will experience pixelation.
Here is the 200 pixel wide image enlarged to fill this page. Notice how fuzzy the pixels are once they are enlarged.

image.alt: Example showing pixelation as a small photo is enlarged.

newpage.pagetitle: Saving Different Versions of a Photo

para.b: Save different versions of a photo by using File/Save As from the menu.

newpage.para: Always maintain your original photos in another folder. Don't save your changes over the top of your originals. That way you can always go back to your originals and re-edit them.
It is a good idea to include the size in the file name to help you keep the different sizes separate. You can view the image size in the title bar.
Click on the + boxes to open up the file browser.
The list of boxes along the top of the file browser shows the "path" through the folders to the current file. Each box is a subfolder of the box on its left.
Go back to the original image and use the same steps to resize the image to 600px wide and save it with the file name: funDayParadeBoy600x893.jpg
Here is a shot of the Layers dialog. I usually move it over to the right side of the screen:

One of the most useful tools for photo editing is the concept of layers. Think of these as clear plastic sheets that allow you to position and edit various elements without affecting the other items in the photo.
This is how photo montages are built. Check out the PhotoShop Contest site (http://photoshopcontest.com/http://photoshopcontest.com/) for some really interesting examples.
Add a new layer click on the "New Layer" icon (blank sheet icon at far left).
Delete a layer by clicking and dragging it to the Trashcan icon or by selecting it and clicking on the Trashcan icon.
On the next page we will be adding text to the image. You will see how GIMP automatically makes a new layer for the text.
To add text to an image, click on the Text tool in the toolbox (Large letter A icon).

para.b: Here is a link to a video showing how to add text.

newpage.para: To change a text properties, make sure the Text tool is selected and highlight the text you want to change. Then, make the changes in the property box.

para.b: Use file/save to save the file as another type.
You can open up the file extensions listing to see a list of different file types to save the file as.

newpage.para: Each file type (.jpg, .gif, and .png) takes the photo and recompresses it using a different compression formula. Because of this you can't just take a .jpg file and rename it to .gif. You have to open it up in a photo editor and resave it as the new format. GIMP will compress the image using the correct compression rules.

You'll also notice, when you use "File/Save As" that you can save the file as a GIMP file (.xsd). This allows you to preserve the layers and other editing tools that GIMP uses. Think of the GIMP files as your working files and the .jpg, .gif, and .png as finished images for the Web.
GIMP Documentationhttp://www.gimp.org/docs/ - A very detailed how-to manual. Lots of screen shots and step-by-step tutorials. http://www.gimp.org/docs/

The Wiki Book About GIMPhttp://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/The_GIMP - an excellent GIMP resource. All the basic techniques are included here, edited by GIMP users around the world. http://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/The_GIMP

para.b: This completes the tutorial: Web Development - GIMP

newpage.para: May 2009
